title: Convolutional Layers & CNN Architecture
summary: Learned FIR filters with hierarchical composition. Weight sharing gives parameter efficiency and cache-friendly GPU execution. Nonlinearity between layers is what makes cascading meaningful. Builds correlations hierarchically (local to global) vs transformer's global-at-once approach. More explainable because each kernel and feature map is directly visualizable.
key_insights: CNN kernels = learned FIR filters. Coefficients learned from data, not hand-designed.
FIRs feeding FIRs: stacked layers = cascaded filter banks. The NONLINEARITY between layers is what prevents collapse to a single longer filter and creates the hierarchy.
CNN builds correlations HIERARCHICALLY (local → composed → global). Transformer discovers them GLOBALLY (all-to-all attention). Two mechanisms for the same goal.
Weight sharing is not just parameter reduction — it creates a cache-friendly memory access pattern that maps perfectly to GPU hardware.
Favorable for low-performance machines: small parameter count, cache-friendly access. But NOT necessarily low-FLOPs — kernel applied at every spatial position.
CNN is more explainable: each kernel is visualizable, each feature map shows where a pattern was detected, the hierarchy itself IS the explanation structure.
Kernels are unconstrained learned basis functions. Can be CONSTRAINED to specific function families (Gabor, wavelet, Chebyshev, Fourier) to encode domain knowledge.
Nonlinearity between conv layers is NOT an MLP — just pointwise ReLU/GELU. Conv = linear transform, activation = nonlinearity. That's the whole unit.

derivations.title: Convolution as constrained matrix multiply (Toeplitz/im2col)

steps.name: Reshape input into patch matrix
steps.math: For input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{\text{in}}}$ with kernel size $k$: extract all $k \times k$ patches, flatten each to $\mathbb{R}^{k^2 C_{\text{in}}}$. Stack as columns: $X_{\text{patches}} \in \mathbb{R}^{k^2 C_{\text{in}} \times N}$ where $N = H_{\text{out}} \cdot W_{\text{out}}$.

steps.name: Kernel as weight matrix
steps.math: Reshape $C_{\text{out}}$ kernels of size $[k, k, C_{\text{in}}]$ into $W \in \mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{out}} \times k^2 C_{\text{in}}}$. Each row is one flattened kernel.

steps.name: Convolution = matrix multiply
steps.math: $Y = W \cdot X_{\text{patches}} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{out}} \times N}$. Reshape back to $[H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}}, C_{\text{out}}]$.

steps.name: Why this is efficient on GPUs
steps.math: Dense GEMM is the most optimized GPU operation. $W$ is small (fits in L1/L2 cache), reused $N$ times. Weight sharing = same weights, millions of reuses, perfect cache locality.

steps.name: Contrast with fully connected
steps.math: FC on same input: $W_{\text{fc}} \in \mathbb{R}^{(C_{\text{out}} \cdot H_{\text{out}} \cdot W_{\text{out}}) \times (C_{\text{in}} \cdot H \cdot W)}$. For $224 \times 224$ RGB → 64 filters: FC ≈ 9.6 billion params. Conv ($3 \times 3$): 1,728 params. Factor of $5.5 \times 10^6$ fewer.

derivations.consequence: Convolution = structured, weight-shared matrix multiply. The Toeplitz structure gives both parameter efficiency and hardware efficiency.

derivations.title: Why nonlinearity between layers is essential — cascade collapse

steps.name: Two linear convolutions in cascade
steps.math: $\mathbf{y} = W_2 (W_1 \mathbf{x}) = (W_2 W_1) \mathbf{x} = W_{\text{combined}} \mathbf{x}$. Cascade of two linear convolutions = single convolution.

steps.name: Effective kernel of the cascade
steps.math: If $W_1$ has kernel size $k_1$ and $W_2$ has kernel size $k_2$, then $W_{\text{combined}}$ has effective kernel size $k_1 + k_2 - 1$. Stacking linear convs only increases receptive field, not expressivity.

steps.name: Nonlinearity breaks the collapse
steps.math: $\mathbf{y} = W_2 \cdot \text{ReLU}(W_1 \mathbf{x})$. Cannot reduce to single linear operation. Output depends on which elements of $W_1 \mathbf{x}$ are positive — input-dependent piecewise-linear behavior.

steps.name: Piecewise linear regions
steps.math: ReLU with $d$ neurons partitions input space into up to $2^d$ regions, each with different linear function. $L$ layers: up to $2^{Ld}$ regions. Exponential growth = source of deep CNN expressivity.

derivations.consequence: Without nonlinearity: $N$ conv layers = 1 conv layer. With nonlinearity: $N$ layers = exponentially many piecewise-linear functions. Nonlinearity is the mechanism making depth meaningful.

derivations.title: Variance evolution through conv layers — why initialization matters

steps.name: Single conv layer variance
steps.math: Output element = sum of $k^2 C_{\text{in}}$ terms, each product of input and weight. $$\text{Var}(\text{output}) = k^2 \cdot C_{\text{in}} \cdot \text{Var}(w) \cdot \text{Var}(x)$$

steps.name: Xavier initialization (variance preservation)
steps.math: For $\text{Var}(\text{output}) = \text{Var}(\text{input})$: $$\text{Var}(w) = \frac{1}{k^2 \cdot C_{\text{in}}}$$

steps.name: After ReLU
steps.math: ReLU zeros negative values. For symmetric zero-mean input, ReLU halves variance: $\text{Var}(\text{after ReLU}) = \frac{1}{2}\text{Var}(\text{input})$

steps.name: Kaiming initialization (corrects for ReLU)
steps.math:
$$\text{Var}(w) = \frac{2}{k^2 \cdot C_{\text{in}}}$$
Factor of 2 compensates for ReLU halving. This is He initialization.

steps.name: Through $L$ layers without correction
steps.math: Variance multiplier per layer = $r$. After $L$ layers: $\text{Var} = \sigma^2 \cdot r^L$. If $r > 1$: explosion. If $r < 1$: collapse. Proper init sets $r = 1$.

derivations.consequence: Same philosophy as $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling in attention: control variance at each stage. Xavier/Kaiming for conv, $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ for attention, LayerNorm for residual streams — all variance control.

derivations.title: Why CNN is efficient for low-performance machines

steps.name: Parameter efficiency
steps.math: Conv: $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}}$ params. For $k=3, C=256$: 590K. FC equivalent: billions. Orders of magnitude smaller.

steps.name: Cache efficiency
steps.math: $W$ fits in GPU cache. Patches accessed sequentially. Near-peak memory bandwidth.

steps.name: But FLOPs are NOT small
steps.math: FLOPs per layer: $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}} H_{\text{out}} W_{\text{out}}$. For $k=3, C=256, 56 \times 56$: 3.6 GFLOPS/layer.

steps.name: Comparison with transformer
steps.math: Attention on $56 \times 56$ input: $O(n^2 d)$ where $n = 3136$. Attention matrix alone: ~10M entries. CNN is cheaper for high-resolution spatial data.

steps.name: Where CNN loses
steps.math: Each layer sees only $k \times k$ neighborhood. Global patterns require many layers, each adding FLOPs. Transformer: global in one layer.

derivations.consequence: CNN = parameter-efficient + cache-efficient, but not FLOP-efficient. Depthwise separable convolutions (MobileNet) address this by factorizing the conv.

derivations.title: Kernels as basis functions — constraining the learned space

steps.name: Unconstrained kernel
steps.math: Standard: $\mathbf{h} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$, all elements free. Maximum flexibility, no domain knowledge.

steps.name: Frequency domain view
steps.math: Convolution in space = multiplication in frequency. $\text{DFT}(\mathbf{h})$ gives frequency response $H(\omega)$. Each kernel is a band-pass, low-pass, or high-pass filter.

steps.name: Gabor-parameterized kernels
steps.math:
$$h(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^2 + \gamma^2 y'^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x'}{\lambda} + \psi\right)$$
Learn only $\theta, \lambda, \psi, \gamma, \sigma$ — 5 params instead of $k^2$.

steps.name: Wavelet-parameterized kernels
steps.math:
$$h(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{x - b}{a}\right)$$
Learn scale $a$ and shift $b$. Mother wavelet $\psi$ is fixed (domain knowledge).

steps.name: General basis function approach
steps.math:
$$\mathbf{h} = \sum_i \alpha_i \, \phi_i$$
where $\phi_i$ are basis functions (Fourier, Chebyshev, Legendre, wavelets). Learn only coefficients $\alpha_i$. Basis encodes structural prior.

steps.name: Hybrid: fixed + free kernels
steps.math: In layer with $C_{\text{out}}$ kernels: fix some to known filters, leave others free. Training only updates free kernels. Choose WHICH patterns the model must detect vs discovers.

derivations.consequence: Kernel parameterization: unconstrained (max flexibility) → basis-constrained (structural priors, fewer params) → fixed (inject known filters). Connects to FOUNDATIONS.INDUCTIVE_BIAS.

key_contrast_with_transformer.cnn_approach: Build correlations HIERARCHICALLY bottom-up. Local features compose through layers. Global emerges from many layers of local composition.
key_contrast_with_transformer.transformer_approach: Discover correlations GLOBALLY all-at-once. Any position attends to any other. No forced hierarchy.
key_contrast_with_transformer.explainability: CNN: each kernel visualizable, feature maps show where patterns detected, layer hierarchy = abstraction hierarchy. Transformer: attention weights visible but FFN internals opaque.
key_contrast_with_transformer.tradeoff: CNN: strong bias = efficient with small data, explainable, forced locality. Transformer: weak bias = needs more data, any pattern discoverable.

signal_processing_bridge.firs_feeding_firs: Cascaded layers = cascaded filter banks with nonlinearities. Nonlinearity prevents collapse, creates abstraction hierarchy.
signal_processing_bridge.kernel_size_as_window: Kernel size $k$ = window length = time-frequency resolution tradeoff (uncertainty principle).
signal_processing_bridge.wavelet_connection: Kernels are wavelet-like (localized, frequency-selective) but unconstrained. Can be constrained to wavelet/Gabor families.
signal_processing_bridge.scattering_transform: Mallat's scattering: fixed wavelet cascade, no learning. Provably translation-invariant, deformation-stable.

geometric_properties: Translation equivariance: shifting input shifts output by same amount (built-in)
Receptive field: $\text{RF} = L(k-1) + 1$ for $L$ layers with kernel $k$
Feature maps as learned basis functions: each kernel = a direction in feature space
Piecewise linear regions from ReLU: input-dependent partitioning of representation space
statistical_properties: Variance evolution: $\text{Var}(\text{out}) = k^2 C_{\text{in}} \cdot \text{Var}(w) \cdot \text{Var}(\text{in})$. Xavier: $\text{Var}(w) = 1/(k^2 C_{\text{in}})$. Kaiming: $\text{Var}(w) = 2/(k^2 C_{\text{in}})$.
Mean drift: ReLU shifts mean positive. Without BatchNorm, means accumulate across layers.
Dead filters: kernels with near-zero activation = wasted capacity.
Weight sharing implies stationarity: same statistics at all spatial positions.
numerical_properties: Params per layer: $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}}$. Much smaller than FC.
FLOPs: $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}} H_{\text{out}} W_{\text{out}}$. NOT small — spatial application.
Cache-friendly: small $W$ reused for all patches. Near-peak bandwidth.
Gradient is also a convolution (transposed) — same efficiency backward.

related: TRANSFORMER.SELF_ATTENTION — hierarchical composition vs global attention
FOUNDATIONS.INDUCTIVE_BIAS — kernel constraints encode domain knowledge
MULTIMODAL.FUSION — CNN as modality-specific feature encoder

notes: Entry point was signal processing analogy — FIR filters, not image classification
Math of convolution itself is simple — depth is in cascading properties and kernel design
Key contrast: CNN builds hierarchy, transformer discovers globally. CNN is more explainable.
Constraining kernels to basis function families is a powerful tool for encoding domain knowledge
Favorable for low-performance machines in parameter count and cache, but FLOPs can still be high
